moment_text: But I remember being a little girl who was, I grew up in the church, and so like if you grew up in the Bible belt, there was a church in every corner. And a lot of times when you didn't get the support you needed from maybe your healthcare provider or community, the church, and that gathering, religious gathering was a way. To connect, but I remember one day it was, I think it was probably after church, my aunt, who was a hard worker, was driving around a cul-de-sac in the neighborhood that we lived in. And I think she was manic. I mean, I will never know for sure. I know she had a history of bipolar, but she drove around that cul-de-sac. I would say for me, it felt like for hours. And I remember asking my grandmother, why is she just driving around the cul-de-sac? Why don't she just like. Park her car and come inside. Uh, when my aunt came in, 'cause I was a curious little girl, I asked her, you know, what was the challenge? Why was she driving around? Was she on the phone? At that time we didn't have cell phones, so obviously it wasn't that, but what was she doing? And she said, I feel like I'm just spinning my wheels. My life feels like I'm working and I'm not getting anywhere. So I wanted to do something about it Now for a little girl where the age I was, and I think I probably was like under seven. That had a profound impact on me. I certainly was proud of my aunt, but to think that someone who worked as hard as she did didn't feel good about the work she was doing. Uh, so I think that actually opened up my eyes to mental health, but also opened up my eyes to the struggle for women. Right. For equity in your life. For the challenges with depression, anxiety. How that may look and manifest itself in real world in action. Granted, you know, later in my life I made the decision to become a psychiatrist, but I really remember that moment and there were other moments after that, uh, that I could share in terms of my childhood journey, but also in my adulthood. But that was a defining moment for me as how life can be so hard sometimes for some people, that it almost feels like there's no escape from it.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains emotional signals (confusion, hopelessness) and describes a system interaction (family). It also contains barrier signals (lack of support).
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker is a clinician, but she is recounting a personal childhood story, so her role is tagged as 'patient' for this lived experience moment.

moment_text: Well, I would say a pivotal moment for me, pre-adolescence. I was almost 10 years old. Uh, and I hate to even start the conversation this way, is that we had a tragedy in my family. You know, certainly I come from a family with a lot of privilege, uh, as it relates to support, but my grandmother had 14 children, right? So if you can imagine that was a lot of activity going on, a lot of family gatherings and fun, but also a lot of pain. Some deep secrets in the family. But one thing that happened that wasn't a secret, and I knew about it, was my uncle who was like a father figure to me, he was brutally murdered by police. The city I lived in, it was a tragedy. It happened actually during what was happening in New York at the time with, I think it's the, uh, they call 'em something else now, the exonerated five or what, whatever that name is. And I was like, oh my gosh. No one ever wanted to talk about the story. There were, the trauma was there, but there was also a lot of fear, uh, and a lot of suspicion about what that would look like if this got out. In terms of my family and the dynamics. And so by the age of 10, I became a new person. Like I was like, I am going to go into the world and change it. Like I'm gonna change how people see me, how they see my family. I'm gonna fix all of these problems. Uh, so that little girl just became someone who became an advocate, a social justice person at heart. It started with, you know, if someone's getting bullied at school, I was the person that was gonna. Speak up against the bully, you know, even though I got bullied myself and that's another story, but you know, I was that person that I would champion other people's causes.
eligibility_rationale: Passes A2 evidence gate: The moment contains strong barrier signals (silence, fear_of_judgment), agency signals (speaking_up), and emotional signals (grief, fear) within a family system interaction.
notes_for_reviewer: The speaker is a clinician, but she is recounting a personal childhood story, so her role is tagged as 'patient' for this lived experience moment.

moment_text: Uh, not recognizing that there was a wound inside of me that needed to heal that internal. Struggle became my superpower, right? I can share the journey, but it's also a deep pain that had to heal, which brought me to becoming a child psychiatrist and learning more about the mental health. There is a personality and attachment and how that works, uh, working with women and children and underserved communities. And that's been most of my work as a clinical educator, uh, an academic, uh, leader. To writing papers about the trauma of social determinants of health, right? Where we work, live, sleep, play love. In some places, our war zones, I don't mean it in the sense that we're under attack, I mean in the sense that our central nervous system, our brain, and our development is under attack because of the scarcity of resources. And I do believe that all roles lead to resources, but some of us are lacking in that support and needing the support from others, uh, to champion for them what equity really looks like.

moment_text: I had the unfortunate of learning that my mom developed breast cancer, and that was a tragedy in itself. She was, at that time, 47 years old, pretty, still pretty young, and it shaped how I thought about what was gonna happen for the next 10, 15 years of her life. And unfortunately, she passed away at the age of 57 from the progression of, uh, me metastatic breast cancer. And, uh, I remember thinking that if someone like her could work so hard. But didn't, didn't get to see the privilege of all the work that she put into because of lack of equity. Because let's face it, many women are not being managed. Their, their healthcare is not being managed appropriately. We're missing opportunity to, to detect breast cancer, but also to prevent it. Uh, and that was a tragedy, uh, for me, but also a reminder that even in our struggle and our resilience, there's time for us to actually really look at what we're doing. In our healthcare setting, how are we treating the patients that we interface with and supporting them in their journey, but also giving them the tools to equip other people as well for future generations.

'cause it wasn't paycheck to paycheck at that time. So whatever, you know, subsidies or money I had was for that. But when Katrina hit New Orleans, everything was lost. You know, I had to start over and I experienced some of that and there was some deep pain associated with. All of those learners. But God equipped me for all of that from day one. So I may be approaching illness or I may just be the weary will, right? But wherever on the continuum I am, there's a mo movement for transformation. moment_text: And so they reached their breaking point. The burnout literature. We talk about the five phases of burnout. moment_text: Uh, and then you get into more of the chronic stress phase, right? And the chronic stress phase is actually where you have the problem. Any stress is good, but chronic stress, oh wow. That ain't true. Black crack, everything cracks. We all crack. Either we crack up with our skin or we crack up, but we're gonna crack up if we're under chronic stress.
